summary: This notebook provides a complete, beginner-friendly pipeline for the Child Mind Institute Problematic Internet Use competition, focusing on ordinal regression. It combines raw tabular features with time-series embeddings extracted via autoencoders, trains multiple tree-based regressors using stratified cross-validation, and optimizes prediction thresholds to maximize the quadratic weighted kappa metric. The author demonstrates how to safely handle categorical variables, compress sequential data into fixed-length vectors, and stabilize predictions through multi-model ensembling. Readers should learn how to integrate learned embeddings into tabular workflows, tune thresholds for ordinal metrics, and combine diverse model outputs to improve leaderboard performance.
competition_flow: Loads tabular and time-series data, extracts autoencoder embeddings for sequential features, merges them with raw tabular inputs, trains an ensemble of tree-based regressors via 5-fold stratified CV, optimizes prediction thresholds for quadratic weighted kappa, and combines multiple model outputs via voting and majority voting to generate a final submission.
data_reading: Reads main tabular data from CSV files using pd.read_csv, and loads time-series data from Parquet files via a custom parallelized function that reads part-0.parquet per directory and computes descriptive statistics (df.describe().values) using ThreadPoolExecutor.
data_processing: Applies StandardScaler before autoencoder input, manually maps categorical 'Season' features to integers using unique value dictionaries, fills missing values with a 'Missing' placeholder before casting to category, drops the 'id' column, and removes rows with missing target values (dropna on 'sii').
features_engineering: Autoencoder embeddings (96 dimensions) extracted from time-series descriptive statistics (dimensionality reduction), manual integer encoding for categorical features, and direct inclusion of raw demographic, physical, fitness, BIA, and survey features.
ensembling: Combines LightGBM, XGBoost, and CatBoost via VotingRegressor, averages predictions across 5 CV folds, optimizes thresholds using scipy.optimize.minimize, and merges three separate submissions via weighted averaging (0.85/0.10/0.05) followed by majority voting across all prediction sets.
insights: Autoencoder embeddings effectively compress time-series statistics into a fixed-length feature vector that improves tabular model performance.
Optimizing prediction thresholds via scipy.optimize.minimize significantly boosts the ordinal regression metric (QWK) compared to simple rounding.
Manual categorical mapping using train-set unique values prevents data leakage between training and test splits.
Combining multiple model outputs via weighted averaging and majority voting stabilizes predictions and yields higher leaderboard scores than individual models.
